main_sections.title: The Day the River Spoke
main_sections.prose_summary: Jahnavi is a bright girl of nearly ten living in a coastal village in India. Her parents work on the farm, and she has been kept home to mind her younger siblings — first Ramu, then little Appu — while her brother Gopi (whom she calls Ettan, meaning 'Elder brother') and neighbour Meena attend school. Sitting by the river one day, weeping silently as a kingfisher swoops and a lizard basks, Jahnavi hears a sleepy, murmuring voice that turns out to be the River itself. The River, gently personified throughout, draws out her sorrow: she has never been allowed to go to school, is frightened it is too late, and yet burns with curiosity — she wants to know why spiders are yellow in yellow flowers, why bamboo trees rustle, why the moon follows the same path, why fish in the field turn to frogs. The River, with quiet wit, reminds her that little girls can do as much as little boys, and advises her simply to slip into the school one morning and see what happens. Jahnavi summons courage and does exactly that, creeping into the back row with sleeping Appu on her shoulder. The teacher notices her, learns she is Gopi's sister, and promises to speak to her father. That evening Jahnavi watches the teacher visit their home; her father scratches his cheek in worry while the teacher persuades him. Her mother, who once longed for school herself but was denied by her own mother, warmly supports Jahnavi's wish, saying girls should learn as much as they want. Jahnavi resolves that when she grows up she will be a teacher, going house to house to bring all the village girls to school. The next morning she runs to the river to share her triumph — 'I did it! I was scared, but I did it!' — and hears the river chuckle and promise to tell her all about the ships that sail the sea. The story is an extract adapted from Kamala Nair's 'The Day the River Spoke'. Its central themes are the right to education, gender equality, self-belief, and the power of nature as both witness and counsel.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. I Close your eyes and quietly listen to the sounds around you for a minute. Share your answers with your classmates and the teacher.

question_bank.stem: Are they trying to tell you something?
Do you think the things around you also listen to you all day long?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. II Jahnavi was a bright young girl who lived with her parents and three brothers in a coastal village in India. Her parents worked on the farm. She had a dream. Share your answers with your classmates and the teacher.

question_bank.stem: What could that dream be?
How could she make her dream come true?
What is your dream? What can you do to make your dream come true?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss (after Section I of the prose). Answer the questions and share with the teacher.

question_bank.stem: What was Jahnavi's dream? Was it important to her? Why?
Do you think the river can help her in fulfilling her dream? How?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss (after Section II of the prose). Complete the table given below with Jahnavi's questions in Column 1 and the River's answers in Column 2. Check your answers with the teacher.

question_bank.stem: Complete the table with Jahnavi's questions and the River's answers.
question_bank.table:
Column 1 (Jahnavi's questions)|Column 2 (River's answers)
...why the moon always comes from behind the hills…?|
|… little girls can do as much as little boys.
|Big boats, so big that they can take hundreds of people, and they sail along the sea with lights that shine all night.
Will they come here?|

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — I. Extract 1: Read the extract 'You shouldn't cry, you know...I've got to hurry to reach the sea, you know.' and answer the questions that follow.

question_bank.stem: Complete the sentence with the correct option from those given in the brackets. The tone of the River in the given extract is __________________. (assuring/sympathising)
Select a phrase from the extract which shows that Jahnavi was a frequent visitor to the spot.

Choose the correct option to complete the sentence. The use of an exclamation mark at the end of the line, 'It couldn't be the river!' expresses __________________. A. excitement  B. hesitation  C. irritation  D. disbelief
Choose three qualities of the River highlighted in the extract from the words in the box given below: affectionate  forgiving  thoughtful  impatient  kind-hearted

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — I. Extract 2: Read the extract 'Can I do something?...They'd chase me out.' and answer the questions that follow.

question_bank.stem: Complete the sentence with an appropriate reason. According to the River, there was no difference between girls and boys because __________________.
Identify whether the following statement is true or false. The River encourages Jahnavi to have faith in herself and fulfill her desire to go to school.

Choose the correct option to complete the sentence. When the River suggests that Jahnavi should 'slip along', it means that she should move __________________. A. quickly  B. casually  C. quietly  D. confidently
What does the repetition of the phrase I couldn't, tell us?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — II. Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: Why does the writer describe different aspects of nature in great detail at the beginning of the story? Why do you think the writer does this?
How did the River know so much about Jahnavi?
Jahnavi says, 'And I'm so old now, they'll never let me go.' What can you infer about Jahnavi and the school from this line?
Why did the River laugh when Jahnavi said she would be scared at school?
Why should Jahnavi follow the River's advice?
How does the conversation between Jahnavi and the River make the story more appealing?
What is the main message that the writer intends to convey?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I Read the words given in the box below. You will listen to five people speak about school life. As you listen, circle the words that are used by the speakers. There are two words you do not need. Words: lawyer  desks  homemaker  principal  posters  newspapers  marriage  advertisements  careers  books
II You will once again listen to the five speakers. As you listen, match each statement 1–7 to each speaker (i)–(v). There are two statements that you do not need.

question_bank.stem: Match each statement to each speaker. Statements: 1. Promotes active participation for all in school activities. 2. Comments on how school makes learning Mathematics joyful. 3. Believes in the power of education that leads to freedom of choice. 4. Shares that school also means companionship. 5. Understands that boys and girls have different abilities. 6. Persuades the parent to grant permission to attend school. 7. Believes that learning skills is not gender-based.

Statement|Speaker No.
1. Promotes active participation for all in school activities|

3. Believes in the power of education that leads to freedom of choice|

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I Read the sentence given below. 'Dear, dear! What's the matter? … You shouldn't cry, you know. And you really shouldn't be scared, when you have been coming here to see me every day, well, almost every day.' The River spoke to Jahnavi in a 'sleepy' voice. Work in pairs and take turns to speak the same sentence in 'other' voices. Some hints are given below: happy  surprised  angry  scared  sad  worried
II Jahnavi asks the River for advice and the River gives advice. Read the given situations and work in pairs to ask for and give advice. Take turns to change your roles. Use the prompts given below.

question_bank.stem: Situation (i): You wish to play football at school but it has an all-boys' team only.
Situation (ii): You have been unable to score well in Mathematics due to nervousness during exams.
Situation (iii): You really enjoy music classes but do not get enough time to practise for it.
Situation (iv): You cannot see the blackboard clearly from the last bench.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Observing Nature. Take some time to observe the world around you. Over the next few days, pay attention to the colours, shapes, patterns, and textures found in nature. Look closely at the plants, trees, flowers, birds, and even the sky. Make detailed notes of what you observe, focusing on textures, movements, and how light plays with these elements. Now, write a descriptive paragraph based on what you've observed. Highlight the colours, shapes, textures, and unique details that made an impression on you. Give a title to your paragraph. Use the Ideas and Prompts table as a guide.

question_bank.stem: Write a descriptive paragraph based on your observation of nature. Give a title to your paragraph. Use the prompts: Every day, I see… / I find it very interesting that… / I like… because… / I feel… as… / I want to tell you that… / My advice to all is…

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I You have learnt that naming words are called nouns and only proper nouns begin with a capital letter. Why has the author used a capital letter for the 'River' even when it is a common noun? (Clue: Has the River been given qualities of a human being?)

question_bank.stem: Why has the author used a capital letter for the 'River' even when it is a common noun? (Clue: Has the River been given qualities of a human being?)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: II There are a few words related to sound in the text. Pick the words from the text and write them next to the correct definition.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: III The word 'catamaran' is used in the text. The word originates from Tamil language 'kattu maram' meaning 'tied wood'. Find out the meanings of these words. From which Indian language these words were borrowed. An example has been given.

question_bank.stem: Find out the meanings of these words and from which Indian language they were borrowed: 1. karma (Sanskrit)  2. sahib  3. chutney  4. guru  5. verandah  6. jungle  7. areca  8. palanquin

tasks_verbatim.task_text: IV Chandu, the fisherman, has a catamaran that the River preferred to a ship. Given below are some boats that are used in different parts of our country: 1. canoe  2. catamaran  3. coracle  4. shikara

question_bank.stem: Which one would you like to travel in?
Make a colourful drawing of a boat that you would like to travel in.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: V Solve the crossword puzzle based on words related to water transport. Across: 1. a large vessel used for transporting goods or passengers by sea  2. a small vessel for travelling on water, typically propelled by oars, sails, or an engine  3. a flat floating structure for travelling across water  4. a boat or ship driven by steam. Down: 5. a small, light, narrow boat, pointed at both ends and moved using a paddle  6. a boat with a powerful engine which can change direction easily and is used to pull large ships into and out of port  7. a boat with sails and sometimes an engine, used for either racing or travelling for pleasure, rhymes with 'cot'

question_bank.stem: Solve the crossword puzzle based on words related to water transport.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: VI Prepositions are words that indicate the relationship between nouns or pronouns and other elements in a sentence. They show direction, location, time or logical relationships between ideas. Now, fill in the blanks with suitable prepositions given within the brackets.

question_bank.stem: Fill in the blanks with suitable prepositions given within the brackets. Jahnavi takes the River's advice and goes to school. On her first day 1. _________ (at/in/on) school, Jahnavi stood nervously 2. _________ (in/outside/above) the gate, clutching her bag close to her. The bell rang and she hurried 3. _________ (outside/through/inside) the bustling classroom. She found a seat 4. _________ (among/between/from) two friendly classmates. The teacher greeted them warmly and started the lesson 5. _________ (about/in/for) numbers. Jahnavi listened attentively, feeling excited 6. _________ (at/about/for) the new adventure ahead.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: VII Words like on, off, up, down can function both as preposition and as adverb. Fill in the blanks with prepositions or adverbs.

question_bank.stem: Fill in the blanks with prepositions or adverbs: 1. The cat jumped ___________ the table. (preposition)  2. The children played ___________ until dark. (adverb)  3. She quickly ran ___________ the park. (preposition)  4. The helicopter was hovering ___________. (adverb)  5. We were just walking ___________, chatting. (adverb)  6. After thoroughly wiping his shoes, he stepped ___________. (adverb)  7. She drove slowly ___________ the sharp curve. (preposition)  8. He placed the keys ___________ the mat before leaving. (preposition)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I Discuss why rivers in India are considered to be sacred and most of them are referred to as female. Some rivers are referred to as male, for example, the Brahmaputra and the Sone.

II Whole Class Activity: 1. India is a multilingual country, and many of us can speak two to three languages easily. Make a list of the languages that everyone in the class can speak and display it on a chart paper in the classroom. 2. In which language do you enjoy reading and learning the most?

question_bank.stem: Make a list of the languages that everyone in the class can speak and display it on a chart paper in the classroom.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: III Read the poster given below and answer the questions that follow. The poster reads: 'The Happiness of a Nation lies in the Dignity of its Daughters'

question_bank.stem: What are the things you can observe in the poster?
Why do you think the girls look happy?
Dignity means 'self-respect'. What is the relation between 'dignity' and 'happiness'?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: IV The Government has launched many schemes for the education of the girl child. Some of them are 'Beti Bachao Beti Padhao', 'Balika Samridhi Yojana', 'Samagra Shiksha Scheme–Kasturba Gandhi Balika Vidyalayas', etc. Find out more such schemes from the internet or from your teacher and spread awareness in your neighbourhood.

main_sections.title: Try Again
main_sections.poem_text:
King Bruce of Scotland flung himself down
In a lonely mood to think;
'Tis true he was monarch, and wore a crown,
But his heart was beginning to sink.

For he had been trying to do a great deed,
To make his people glad;
He had tried and tried, but couldn't succeed
And so he became quite sad.

He flung himself down in low despair,
As grieved as man could be;
And after a while he pondered there,
"I'll give it all up," said he.

Now just at that moment a spider dropped,
With its silken, filmy clue;
And the King, in the midst of his thinking, stopped
To see what the spider would do.

'Twas a long way up to the ceiling dome,
And it hung by a rope so fine;
That how it would get to its cobweb home,
King Bruce could not divine.

It soon began to cling and crawl
Straight up with strong endeavour;
But down it came with a slippery sprawl,
As near to the ground as ever.

Up, up it ran, not a second to stay,
To utter the least complaint;
Till it fell still lower, and there it lay,
A little dizzy and faint.

Its head grew steady — again it went,
And travelled a half-yard higher;
'Twas a delicate thread it had to tread,
And a road where its feet would tire.

Again it fell and swung below,
But again it quickly mounted;
Till up and down, now fast, now slow,
Nine brave attempts were counted.

"Sure," cried the King, "that foolish thing
Will strive no more to climb;
When it toils so hard to reach and cling,
And tumbles every time."

But up the insect went once more,
Ah me! 'tis an anxious minute;
He's only a foot from his cobweb door,
Oh say, will he lose or win it?

Steadily, steadily, inch by inch,
Higher and higher he got;
And a bold little run at the very last pinch
Put him into his native cot.

"Bravo, bravo!" the King cried out,
"All honour to those who try;
The spider up there, defied despair;
He conquered, and why shouldn't I?"

And Bruce of Scotland braced his mind,
And gossips tell the tale,
That he tried once more as he tried before,
And that time did not fail.

Eliza Cook
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: Written by Eliza Cook in a narrative ballad style with an ABAB rhyme scheme, 'Try Again' recounts the legend of King Bruce of Scotland who, despairing after repeated failures to accomplish a great deed for his people, watches a spider attempt nine times to climb to its cobweb home before finally succeeding. The spider serves as an extended metaphor for perseverance — its every stumble and recovery mirroring Bruce's own repeated defeats. The dominant tone is one of quiet, mounting suspense that breaks into celebration and renewed resolve. Central imagery includes the 'silken, filmy clue' of the spider's thread, the 'ceiling dome' as an aspirational height, and 'steadily, steadily, inch by inch' as the rhythm of determined effort. The poem's chief device is repetition — 'tried and tried', 'up, up', 'steadily, steadily' — which enacts the very persistence it celebrates. The closing stanza resolves both story threads: Bruce, inspired by the creature, tries once more and succeeds. The universal message is that failure is not final; perseverance converts despair into triumph.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — I. Complete the summary of the poem by filling in the blanks with suitable words from the text given in the box below. Box: tried  mounted  despair  attempts  great  conquered  low  strive

question_bank.stem: Complete the summary of the poem by filling in the blanks with suitable words from the box: The poem, written in a narrative style, tells the story of King Bruce of Scotland, who was feeling 1. _____________ after failing multiple times to achieve something 2. _____________ for his people. In a moment of 3. _____________, he observed a spider trying to reach its web high above. The spider 4. _____________ every time it fell after getting close to its goal. It made nine 5. _____________ and finally succeeded. The king said that the spider had 6. _____________ and so would he. Inspired by the spider's determination, King Bruce decided to 7. _____________. When he 8. _____________ this time, he ultimately succeeded.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — II. Complete the following sentences suitably.

question_bank.stem: We can say that the poet uses the narrative style because ________.
The central idea of the poem is ________.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — III. Pick three examples from the poem for each of the following.

question_bank.stem: Pick three examples from the poem for: lines that describe the spider's efforts.
Pick three examples from the poem for: alliteration (the repetition of the same consonant sounds at the beginning of closely connected words).

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — IV. Why does the poet repeat the following words or phrases in the poem? tried and tried; steadily, steadily; up, up

question_bank.stem: Why does the poet repeat the following words or phrases in the poem? tried and tried; steadily, steadily; up, up

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — V. Fill in the blanks by choosing the correct answer from within the brackets.

question_bank.stem: The rhyme scheme of the poem is ____________. (AABB/ABBA/ABAB)
'Bravo' is an example of ____________. (conjunction/interjection/adjective)
The poet uses 'twas and 'tis for the sake of ____________. (rhythm/rhyme/contraction)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — VI.1. A metaphor is a figure of speech that involves an implied comparison between two unlike things without using 'like', 'as', or 'as...as'. The spider's climbing is compared to King Bruce's own struggles — this poetic device is called metaphor. Read the following sentences and identify metaphors and similes. Write M for metaphor and S for simile.

question_bank.stem: Read the following sentences and identify metaphors and similes. Write M for metaphor and S for simile: (i) He ran as fast as a cheetah. (ii) The world is a stage. (iii) The night was as dark as coal. (iv) He sang like an angel. (v) Her voice was music to his ears. (vi) The classroom was a zoo. (vii) The baby slept like a log. (viii) Her smile was as bright as the sun. (ix) The lake was a mirror, reflecting the sky.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — VI.2. Read the following sentences and transform the similes to metaphors and metaphors to similes. Two examples have been given.

question_bank.stem: Transform the similes to metaphors and metaphors to similes: (iii) The book was a treasure chest, filled with endless adventures. (iv) Her laughter was a melody that brightened the darkest days. (v) The mountain stood as a guard, watching over the valley below. (vi) The night was like a velvet cloak, wrapping the world in mystery. (vii) Her thoughts were like butterflies in a summer meadow.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — I. Extract 1: 'He flung himself down in low despair...With its silken, filmy clue;' Answer the questions that follow.

question_bank.stem: Select the phrase which shows the physical expression of the King's emotional state.
Why does the poet use the word 'low' before despair?
Choose the correct option to complete the sentence. In the line, 'And after a while he pondered there', the word 'pondered' means _____________________. A. paused  B. thought  C. noticed  D. rested
Choose the correct option from the words given in the brackets. The phrase 'silken filmy clue' creates an image of something that is _____________________ (long and light/very delicate and soft).

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — I. Extract 2: '...when it toils so hard to reach and cling, And tumbles every time.' / 'But up the insect went once more...' Answer the questions that follow.

question_bank.stem: Choose the correct option to complete the analogy. toil: hard::_______________: _______________ A. roll: tumble  B. tumble: circle  C. compress: roll  D. jump: tumble
List any two characteristics of the spider highlighted in the line, 'But up the insect went once more'.
Choose the correct option to complete the sentence. The expression 'Ah me!' in the extract indicates a sense of _______________. A. happiness  B. stress  C. relief  D. loneliness
Complete the sentence with an appropriate reason. Readers are able to relate to this extract because ____________.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — II. Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: How does the first stanza help in setting the mood of the poem?
Describe how King Bruce's attitude changes from the beginning towards the end of the poem.
The poet describes every action of the spider in great detail. What does this tell us about his attitude towards the spider?
How does the spider inspire us to overcome despair and not give up?
The poem teaches us that failures are stepping stones to success. Explain.
The spider's journey tells us that anyone can be a source of inspiration in our lives. Elaborate.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I You will listen to a story about an ant. Read the questions given below and before you listen, guess the answers to these questions.

question_bank.stem: What was the ant known for? (i) determination  (ii) strength  (iii) wisdom
Why was the ant unable to take the roti home? (i) It was stuck under a rock.  (ii) It was too heavy to carry.  (iii) It was pulled by other ants.
options: It was stuck under a rock.
It was too heavy to carry.
It was pulled by other ants.

question_bank.stem: What was the bird doing? (i) playing with the ant  (ii) searching for food  (iii) picking up leaves
How did the bird make the ant feel? (i) proud  (ii) inspired  (iii) relaxed
How did the fellow ants help? (i) by advising the ant continuously  (ii) by standing still near the ant  (iii) by joining the ant in her efforts
options: by standing still near the ant
by joining the ant in her efforts

tasks_verbatim.task_text: II Now, listen to the story once again and as you listen, check whether your answers are correct.

Narrate a personal experience about a time when you were motivated by someone or something to carry on and never give up. Your perseverance helped you. Use the following sentence prompts as a guide for your narration: 1. Introduction  2. Describe the challenge  3. Feeling disheartened  4. The source of motivation  5. Taking action  6. The result  7. Reflection.

question_bank.stem: Narrate a personal experience about a time when you were motivated by someone or something to carry on and never give up. Use the sentence prompts as a guide.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: King Bruce was inspired by the spider that kept on trying till it was successful. Think about the steps you can take to overcome a difficult situation and write them down. Now, write a letter to your cousin on how you plan to overcome any difficult situation. Also, advise not to quit and persevere. Remember to use transition words like 'To begin with…,' 'Next…,' 'After that…,' 'Then...,' 'Finally...,' to share the steps of your plan.

question_bank.stem: Write a letter to your cousin on how you plan to overcome any difficult situation. Advise not to quit and persevere. Use transition words: 'To begin with…,' 'Next…,' 'After that…,' 'Then...,' 'Finally...' Begin: '20, Rajendra Nagar / Jeevanpur / 23 August 20XX / Dear Monika, / Thank you for your letter asking me about…' End: 'Yours affectionately, / Deepa'

tasks_verbatim.task_text: II Did you know that spiders are not insects? They belong to a group called the arachnids. Observe a spider and an ant from a distance and note down the differences between them. Share your observations with your classmates. Ask your Science teacher for more information to know about spiders better.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: III Let us learn how to weave a spider web. 1. Take three sticks (each about eight inches long) and a roll of old wool. 2. Tie the sticks together in the centre with the wool (Fig. 1). 3. Start weaving the web by putting a loop around every stick (Figs. 2 and 3). 4. Complete the web and tie the thread in a knot on the stick where the web is complete (Fig. 4). Now, decorate it with words and phrases you learnt about the spider in the poem or anything else that you like.

question_bank.stem: Weave a spider web using sticks and wool following the four steps given. Decorate it with words and phrases from the poem.

main_sections.title: Three Days to See
main_sections.prose_summary: This essay is an adapted extract from Helen Keller's celebrated personal essay in which she, a blind and deaf author, reflects on what she would most like to see if given sight for just three days. The piece opens with Keller describing the rich sensory world she accesses through touch alone — the delicate symmetry of a leaf, the smooth bark of a silver birch versus the shaggy bark of a pine, the quiver of a singing bird felt through a small tree. Yet touch intensifies rather than satisfies her longing: if so much beauty is available without sight, how much more must vision reveal? She imagines dividing three days of sight into three parts. On the first day she would see the people whose kindness and companionship have made her life worth living; unable to look into friends' eyes, she currently knows them only by the feel of their faces and can detect broad emotions but not the subtleties the eye communicates. On the second day she would arise with the dawn, witness the miracle of night turning to day, and then devote the hours to a hasty glimpse of the world's history — visiting museums to see the condensed pageant of humankind's progress, including the remains of prehistoric creatures. On the third day she would return to the living world — standing at a busy city corner merely watching people going about their lives, reading smiles and suffering and determination on their faces. At midnight sight would close again. She closes with a passionate appeal to sighted readers: use your eyes as if tomorrow you might be struck blind; apply the same urgency to hearing, touch, smell, and taste. Of all the senses, she concludes, sight must be the most delightful. The essay's tone moves from quiet wonder and poignant longing to inspiring advocacy for mindful, grateful engagement with the world.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. I Work in pairs. Recall a visit to a place that you liked. Discuss the details of that place and write about the following: 1. Name of the place  2. The person(s) you went with  3. The things that you could: see / listen / smell / taste / touch and feel

question_bank.stem: Recall a visit to a place that you liked. Discuss the details and write: 1. Name of the place  2. The person(s) you went with  3. The things that you could: see / listen / smell / taste / touch and feel

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. II How does this memory make you feel? III Do you think your visit/experience would be different if you could not see or hear? Share your answers with your classmates and the teacher.

question_bank.stem: How does this memory make you feel?
Do you think your visit/experience would be different if you could not see or hear?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — I. Work in pairs. Identify the statements that are true from the ones given below. Share your answers with the teacher.

question_bank.stem: Identify the statements that are true: 1. The author expresses a deep longing to experience the world through the sense of hearing. 2. The author would like exploring the city only to observe how people are suffering. 3. The author would spend the first day seeing the people whose kind-heartedness and friendship have enriched her life. 4. The author acknowledges that even in three days, she would not be able to see everything. 5. The author feels that everyone can find the greatest joy merely by the sense of touch. 6. The author advises those who can see to appreciate every moment as if they might lose their senses tomorrow. 7. On the third day, the author would like to observe people in their daily lives and understand their experiences.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — II. Complete the table given below with what Helen Keller wanted to do on the three days if she had sight.

question_bank.stem: Complete the table with what Helen Keller wanted to do on the three days if she had sight.
question_bank.table:
Day 1|Day 2|Day 3
see the people, know her friends from the feel of their faces, …|arise with the dawn, …|

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — III. The author says that you need eyes to be able to 'see' the true self of a person. Therefore, she refers to the eye as 'window of the soul'. How is the narrator able to understand the feelings of a person?

question_bank.stem: How is the narrator able to understand the feelings of a person?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — I. Extract 1: 'I feel the delicate symmetry of a leaf...feel the happy quiver of a bird in full song.' Answer the questions that follow.

question_bank.stem: What does the 'delicate symmetry of a leaf' symbolise?
List two phrases from the extract that describe the texture of objects in nature.
Complete the sentence appropriately. The phrase 'awakening Nature after her winter's sleep' refers to __________________.
What is the tone of the writer in this extract? A. nostalgic  B. proud  C. admiring  D. celebratory

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — I. Extract 2: 'The next day I should arise with the dawn...and so I should go to the museums.' Answer the questions that follow.

question_bank.stem: The word 'thrilling' has been used with 'miracle'. Which of the following words does not match with the word 'thrilling'? A. experience  B. tale  C. knowledge  D. adventure
Why does the writer refer to the earth as 'sleeping earth'?
Complete the sentence with an appropriate reason. The writer wishes to dedicate the day to a brief look at the present and past world because __________________.
Why does the writer use 'should' multiple times in the extract?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — II. Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: The sense of touch makes up for the loss of sight and hearing. Explain this statement with reference to the text.
Why does the author believe that the sense of sight is the most wonderful?
How might the author's opinion on making the most of our senses guide us to be kinder towards people with special abilities?
What is the significance of imagining the loss of a sense, according to the author?
How does the author encourage people to approach their everyday sensory experiences?
What do the author's choices for the three days tell us about her values and priorities?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a conversation between a mother and son. As you listen, select four true statements from 1–7 given below.

question_bank.stem: Select four true statements: 1. Anuj finds the concept of Braille fascinating and wants to learn more about it. 2. Mother is unsure of the school's efforts to include students with visual impairment. 3. Anuj initially thinks that Digital India is only about learning how to use computers. 4. Mother believes that screen readers are very effective for persons who are visually impaired. 5. Anuj already knew that persons who are visually impaired can use computers and smartphones. 6. Anuj wants to know about the effectiveness of assistive technologies for persons who are visually impaired. 7. Mother believes a right mindset is the best support for persons who are visually impaired.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Work in pairs. Take turns to speak for a minute on any one of the senses you value the most. Use the given prompts to frame your response before you speak.

question_bank.stem: Speak for a minute on any one of the senses you value the most using the prompts: I value my sense of sight/ sound/ touch/ smell / taste the most because… / One of my favourite experiences is… / I appreciate being able to see/ hear/touch/ smell/ taste… / God forbid, if I were to lose the sense of sight/ sound/ touch/ smell / taste, it would make me feel… / I would not be able to… / I thank God that I am able to see/ hear/ touch/ smell / taste.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: A descriptive paragraph describes a person, place, object or event to create a memorable experience for the reader. Write a descriptive paragraph describing a place that you visited recently. Follow the guidelines given below: 1. In the opening line, begin with something interesting to capture the reader's attention. Introduce the place you visited. 2. In the supporting lines, describe the place using adjectives and specific details that appealed to your five senses—sight, sound, smell, taste, and touch. You may include alliterations, similes, and personifications. 3. In the concluding lines, summarise the description to provide a final impression.

question_bank.stem: Write a descriptive paragraph describing a place that you visited recently following the three-part structure: (1) opening line to capture attention, (2) supporting lines using sensory details — sight, sound, smell, taste, touch — with alliterations, similes and personifications, (3) concluding lines with a final impression.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I Sensory words are descriptive — they describe how we experience the world. Complete the following table with sensory words from the text in Column 2 for the five senses in Column 1. Add new words in Column 3.

question_bank.stem: Complete the table with sensory words from the text and add new words in Column 3.
question_bank.table:
Column 1 (Sense)|Column 2 (From text)|Column 3 (New words)
What you see|panorama|
What you hear|music|
What you smell|perfume|
What you taste|relish|
What you feel|rough|

tasks_verbatim.task_text: II Read the following sentences from the text and underline the verbs. Modal verbs are auxiliary verbs that express necessity, possibility, permission, or ability. III Identify the modal verbs in the following sentences. Choose the functions they express from those given in the box: possibility  necessity  polite request  ability  moral obligation

question_bank.stem: Identify the modal verbs in the following sentences and choose the functions they express from the box (possibility / necessity / polite request / ability / moral obligation): 1. You can learn a lot from this experience. 2. They might arrive late due to traffic. 3. She must finish her homework before dinner. 4. They ought to apologise for their mistake. 5. He would like to have some lassi, please.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: IV Read the situations in Column 1 and functions in Column 2. Fill in the blanks in the sentences in Column 4 with appropriate modal verbs from Column 3. After filling in the blanks, explain the function of each modal verb to your classmates and the teacher.

question_bank.stem: Fill in the blanks with appropriate modal verbs: (i) ____________ I leave early today if I finish all my tasks? [permission] (ii) I ____________ finish this report by tomorrow. [obligation] (iii) You ____________ consider all the pros and cons before making a decision. [advice] (iv) It ____________ rain later today. [possibility] (v) ____________ you pass the salt, please? [polite request] (vi) You ____________ apologise for the mistake you made. [moral obligation] (vii) We ____________ go to any park nearby for picnic. [suggestion]

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I Do you know how persons with visual challenges are able to read and write? They read through touch with the help of Braille. Study the Braille code given below and write your name using it. You may use bindis or grains of daal to make the dots that you can touch and feel.

question_bank.stem: Study the Braille code and write your name using it. You may use bindis or grains of daal to make the dots that you can touch and feel.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: II Have you heard about the International Day of Persons with Disabilities? Find out when and how it is observed in India. Write your findings on a sheet and put it up on the class board.

question_bank.stem: Find out when and how the International Day of Persons with Disabilities is observed in India. Write your findings on a sheet and put it up on the class board.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: III Do you want to know about sign language? Go to the link given below to learn about the Indian sign language. https://ncert.nic.in/pdf/accessibility/ISL_200word_v15.pdf

question_bank.stem: Go to the link to learn about the Indian sign language: https://ncert.nic.in/pdf/accessibility/ISL_200word_v15.pdf
